completeness.rationale: Extracted most expected information: ~5,000 tasks, slow on description search vs fast on title search, v2.3 upgrade correlation, ~2 weeks timing, 10-15 second delays, and long descriptions. Missed CPU spike detail, but that wasn't volunteered and would have required a specific follow-up question.

accuracy.rationale: The root cause hypothesis is remarkably accurate — it correctly identifies the likely switch from indexed search to naive LIKE/substring scanning, the missing or dropped full-text index on descriptions, and that the title search path remains indexed. This matches the actual cause (FTS5 index replaced by LIKE query) almost exactly.

efficiency.rationale: Only 2 agent turns to reach a comprehensive and accurate triage summary. Every question was well-targeted. The first question established the baseline and timeline, the second elicited reproduction details and task count. The reporter volunteered the critical title-vs-description distinction in response to well-framed questions.

question_quality.rationale: Questions were well-structured and diagnostic. The first question efficiently targeted both baseline performance and timeline. The second bundled reproduction steps, scale, and query patterns effectively. The multi-part format in the second question successfully prompted the reporter to think critically and discover the title-vs-description distinction themselves. Could have asked about CPU/resource usage or system monitoring to get the CPU spike detail.

actionability.rationale: The summary is immediately actionable: it identifies the specific version, the specific feature (description search), provides a clear root cause hypothesis pointing to migration scripts and index changes, includes concrete debugging steps (EXPLAIN/ANALYZE), reproduction steps with specific numbers, and even a proposed test case with acceptance criteria. A developer could start investigating within minutes.

notable_strengths: Extremely efficient — reached a high-quality conclusion in just 2 turns
Root cause hypothesis almost perfectly matches the actual cause without seeing any code
The second question's structure prompted the reporter to self-discover the critical title-vs-description distinction
Recommended fix steps are concrete and correctly ordered for investigation
Proposed test case is specific and regression-oriented
notable_weaknesses: Did not capture CPU spike behavior during searches
Environment details are incomplete — dismissed OS/hardware as 'likely irrelevant' rather than capturing what was available
Did not ask whether the reporter had tried any workarounds or troubleshooting steps
most_insightful_question: Does every search take 10-15 seconds, or only certain queries? — This question directly led the reporter to test and discover that title search was fast while description search was slow, which is the key diagnostic finding.
missed_opportunities: Could have asked about system resource usage during slow searches (would have revealed CPU spike)
Could have asked about the specific upgrade path — clean install vs migration — which is relevant to whether the FTS index was properly migrated
Could have asked if the reporter had tried reindexing or clearing cache, which would have confirmed the index hypothesis
